sections.heading: The national level changed little, but the regional map shifted
sections.body: Postnatal checks during the first 2 days after birth were recorded for 76.5 percent of live births and stillbirths in 2025, close to the 75.5 percent level in 2022. That national stability hides much larger regional movement. The 2025 regional range runs from 91.4 percent in Western Visayas to 54.1 percent in BARMM, a 37.3-point spread. The indicator is therefore less about a broad national swing than about where early checks are consistently reaching mothers and newborns.

sections.heading: Western Visayas and Davao form the upper end of the 2025 distribution
sections.body: Western Visayas had the highest 2025 level at 91.4 percent, followed by Davao Region at 89.0 percent. Northern Mindanao and Caraga were also high at 86.5 percent and 86.2 percent, respectively. These regions are well above the national level of 76.5 percent. The upper end is not confined to one island group, which points to a more scattered pattern of strong early postnatal contact.

sections.heading: The lowest regional levels are far below the national average
sections.body: BARMM was lowest in 2025 at 54.1 percent, followed by Cagayan Valley at 61.2 percent and MIMAROPA at 62.0 percent. Each of these regions was more than 14 points below the national level. The distance from the highest region, Western Visayas, to BARMM is especially large at 37.3 points. That gap matters because the indicator covers checks in the first 2 days, when missed contact can mean missed early support for both mothers and newborns.

sections.heading: Some regions moved sharply in opposite directions
sections.body: Ilocos Region rose from 62.6 percent in 2022 to 81.1 percent in 2025, the largest increase among regions with comparable data. BARMM also improved, from 44.5 percent to 54.1 percent, but it remained at the bottom of the 2025 distribution. Cagayan Valley moved the other way, dropping from 79.9 percent to 61.2 percent, while MIMAROPA fell from 75.8 percent to 62.0 percent. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ also declined, from 79.0 percent to 72.3 percent, placing it below the national level in 2025.

sections.heading: The combined total is essentially unchanged from 2022
sections.body: For live births and stillbirths combined, the postnatal check level was 76.5 percent in 2025. The comparable 2022 level was 75.5 percent, a 1.0-point increase. This is a small national change, especially compared with the regional shifts. The total therefore gives a stable headline, while the subgroup tables show where the remaining variation sits.

sections.heading: Younger mothers remain slightly behind, even after improvement
sections.body: Mothers under age 20 had a postnatal check level of 73.0 percent in 2025, below 77.0 percent among mothers age 20 to 34 and 76.7 percent among mothers age 35 to 49. The under-20 group improved from 67.5 percent in 2022, a 5.5-point increase. Even with that gain, it remained the lowest of the three age groups in 2025. The age pattern is modest, but it points to younger mothers as the group with the clearest remaining disadvantage.

sections.heading: Urban and rural levels are almost the same
sections.body: Urban mothers had a 2025 postnatal check level of 77.2 percent, compared with 75.9 percent among rural mothers. The 1.3-point gap is small and both groups increased slightly from 2022. Rural areas rose by 1.3 points, while urban areas rose by 1.0 point. Residence alone does not explain the larger differences seen across regions.

sections.heading: Education shows a wider split than residence
sections.body: Postnatal checks were highest among mothers with Grades 11 to 12 education at 81.8 percent in 2025. The level was 79.0 percent among college-educated mothers and 77.8 percent among those with post-secondary education. Mothers with Grades 1 to 6 education were lower at 68.7 percent, while the no-education row was 54.2 percent and is shown with a caution marker in the source. The education pattern is therefore more visible than the urban-rural split, though the smallest education category should be read carefully.

sections.heading: Wealth differences are present but not steep
sections.body: The lowest wealth quintile had the lowest 2025 level at 71.6 percent. The middle quintile was highest at 79.4 percent, followed closely by the second quintile at 79.0 percent and the fourth quintile at 78.5 percent. The highest quintile was 75.0 percent, below the middle groups rather than clearly above them. This makes the wealth pattern uneven rather than a simple climb from poorest to richest.

takeaways: Postnatal checks during the first 2 days after birth were nearly unchanged nationally, at 76.5 percent in 2025 for live births and stillbirths combined.
Regional differences are much larger than the national change, ranging from 91.4 percent in Western Visayas to 54.1 percent in BARMM.
Ilocos Region improved sharply from 2022, while Cagayan Valley and MIMAROPA fell well below their earlier levels.
Urban-rural differences are small, but younger mothers, mothers with less schooling, and the lowest wealth quintile remain lower than several comparison groups.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
